Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines sources that are fetched from official AWS domains (awscli.amazonaws.com and raw.githubusercontent.com/aws/aws-cli) using HTTPS, which is a trusted and expected upstream for this package. The `source` array entries use the package version `${pkgver}` in the URLs, but this is standard practice and does not introduce dynamic code execution during source fetching. The checksums are provided and not set to 'SKIP', meaning integrity is enforced. The top-level scope contains no command substitutions, function calls, or immediate execution of downloaded content. The `pkgver()` function is absent, so no dynamic version calculation occurs at parse time. All variables are statically defined. The commands `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will only evaluate the global scope and extract sources, none of which involve executing or sourcing untrusted code during this phase. The `package()` function is irrelevant here due to `--noprepare` and `--nobuild`, so its operations are not executed.
</details>
<summary>
Only static sources from trusted AWS domains; no runtime code execution on parse.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Only static sources from trusted AWS domains; no runtime code execution on parse.[/]

LLM auditresponse for aws-cli-v2-bin.install:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The `aws-cli-v2-bin.install` script contains only a `post_upgrade()` function that checks if the previous version of the package is older than or equal to 2.13.25. If so, it displays a message informing users that zsh completions are no longer automatically enabled and instructs them to manually source `/usr/bin/aws_zsh_completer.sh` if needed.

This behavior is expected and benign. It is a standard packaging practice to provide migration guidance during upgrades, especially when a configuration or usability change affects user experience. The script does not perform any network operations, execute external code, modify system files beyond standard package installation, or handle sensitive data. No obfuscated or dangerous commands (e.g., eval, curl|bash, base64 decoding) are present.

The message is purely informational and consistent with typical AUR package maintenance. There is no evidence of malicious intent, backdoors, or supply-chain tampering.
</details>
<summary>
Informs about zsh completion change; no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/3] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed aws-cli-v2-bin.install. Status: SAFE -- Informs about zsh completion change; no malicious behavior
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD retrieves the AWS CLI v2 binary directly from Amazon's official distribution endpoint at `awscli.amazonaws.com`, which is the canonical and trusted source for this software, as verified by AWS documentation. All source URLs point to the project's own upstream (github.com/aws/aws-cli or awscli.amazonaws.com), and checksums are provided for all architecture-specific binaries and auxiliary files. The PGP key listed in `validpgpkeys` matches the one documented by AWS, although no signature verification step is present in the build process — a minor hygiene concern, but not a vulnerability given that the file integrity is ensured via `sha256sums`.

The package installation routine is straightforward and follows standard practices: it installs the binary, creates symlinks in /usr/bin, deploys shell completion scripts, and installs the license. No unexpected or dangerous commands (e.g., `eval`, `curl|bash`, obfuscated scripts) are used, and there are no network operations during the build. The `options=(!strip !debug)` directive disables debugging and stripping, which is acceptable for a binary-only package. No file operations reach outside the package directory or interact with user data.

While the missing PGP signature verification reduces assurance compared to a fully verified build, this does not constitute malicious behavior. The sources are from trusted domains, SHA-256 sums are present and properly pinned, and the operations performed are consistent with standard AUR packaging for a binary release. No telemetry, backdoors, or unauthorized data exfiltration mechanisms are introduced by the PKGBUILD.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary packaging with proper checksums; no malicious code</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for aws_bash_completer:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `aws_bash_completer` contains a single line of shell code: `complete -C aws_completer aws`. This line registers command-line completion for the `aws` CLI tool by instructing bash to use the external program `aws_completer` to dynamically provide completions.

There is no network activity, file manipulation, code execution, or obfuscation present. The content is a standard bash completion directive commonly used in shell environments. The referenced `aws_completer` is expected to be installed alongside the AWS CLI and is part of its official functionality.

The file does not perform any harmful or suspicious actions. It aligns with standard packaging practices for CLI tools requiring tab completion. No malicious behavior or supply-chain attack vectors are present.
</details>
<summary>
Standard bash completion script, no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for aws_zsh_completer.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `aws_zsh_completer.sh` script is designed to enable Zsh shell completion for the `aws` command by leveraging Zsh's `bashcompinit` function, which provides compatibility with Bash-style completion. The script defines `_bash_complete()` to set up environment variables like `COMP_LINE`, `COMP_POINT`, and `COMP_WORDS` that are expected by the AWS CLI completion script, and then uses `complete -C aws_completer aws` to delegate completion to the `aws_completer` script. This is standard and expected behavior for enabling cross-shell completion support.

There are no signs of malicious activity. The code does not perform network operations, file exfiltration, or execute dynamically downloaded content. It does not use `eval`, `curl`, `wget`, or other dangerous constructs in a suspicious context. The script operates entirely within the shell environment and manipulates only shell variables and completion functions. The note about Zsh versions prior to a specific commit refers to a legitimate limitation in older Zsh versions regarding environment variable export during completion — this is not obfuscation or a backdoor.

All operations are transparent, focused solely on shell completion functionality, and consistent with standard shell scripting practices for enhancing user experience with the AWS CLI. No data is sent outside the system, and no system modifications occur beyond temporary shell function definitions.
</details>
<summary>
Legitimate shell completion script, no malicious behavior
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD follows standard AUR packaging practices for a binary release of the official AWS CLI v2. All source URLs point to the project's official upstream locations:
- Completions and license from `raw.githubusercontent.com/aws/aws-cli/` using the specified version tag.
- Binary archive from `awscli.amazonaws.com`, Amazon's official distribution endpoint.

All downloads are pinned to a specific version (`${pkgver}=2.35.7`) and include explicit `sha256sums` (no `SKIP`). The `package()` function performs only routine installation operations: creating directories, moving pre-built binaries, installing documentation files, and creating symlinks into `/usr/bin`. There are no network requests, no execution of downloaded code (beyond the normal `makepkg` source extraction), no obfuscated commands, and no manipulation of files outside the package's own installation prefix (`/usr/local/aws-cli` and `/usr/bin`). The declared `validpgpkeys` is listed but not used for signature verification; this is a best-practice omission (the binary is not signed) but not evidence of malice. No code is injected into the build process beyond the upstream content. The file is consistent with legitimate packaging of a trusted upstream binary.
</details>
<summary>
Legitimate PKGBUILD for official AWS CLI binary, no malicious content.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `aws_zsh_completer.sh` is a standard completion helper script for the AWS CLI, enabling zsh bash compatibility for auto-completion. It defines a `_bash_complete` function that replicates bash completion logic within zsh and registers `aws_completer` as the completer for the `aws` command via `complete -C`. There are no suspicious network requests, no obfuscated code, no base64 decoding, no eval of arbitrary commands, and no file exfiltration or system modifications outside the scope of providing shell completions. The script follows expected packaging practices for providing interactive shell features.
</details>
<summary>A standard zsh completion script for AWS CLI.</summary>
</security_assessment>

The page is consistent with legitimate upstream AWS CLI behavior and contains no obfuscation, encoded commands, or unexpected operations. There is no evidence of a supply‑chain attack in this file.
